dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for a sick person near the Boxwood building.
Hi, my name is Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
We received a dispatch that someone is unwell here. Can you tell me what's going on?
I've had left side pain and shortness of breath for about three days now.
She's been having this for the last few days and it just got worse today.
Ma'am, can you tell me your name?
It's Sarah Thompson.
What is the date today, and the month three months ago?
August 8th. Three months ago was May.
Where are we right now?
In the lobby of the Boxwood building.
Why are you not feeling well today?
I have pain on my left side and I can't catch my breath.
Your eyes are open and alert.
We'll start with your airway, Mrs. Thompson. Ma'am, can you speak clearly?
Yes, I can.
Your airway appears to be open and clear. No signs of obstruction.
Mrs. Thompson, can you take a deep breath for me, just to help us assess?
Ugh, it hurts a little but yes.
Respirations are 24 per minute, shallow effort noted.
Mrs. Thompson, we're going to listen to your lungs now. Is that all right?
Yes, I suppose so.
Bilateral wheezing present on expiration, more pronounced on the left.
She has signs of mild respiratory distress but is breathing consistently.
Oxygen saturation is 94% on room air. That's slightly decreased.
Pulse is 75, strong and regular radial pulse noted.
Blood pressure is 174/79. Let me confirm that again in a moment.
The skin is pink, warm, and dry. Mild edema is present in both ankles.
Mrs. Thompson, can you walk me through when the pain and shortness of breath began?
The pain originally resembled a UTI symptoms three days ago, and it's gotten progressively worse.
She was in the hospital last week with the same symptoms and was given antibiotics and an inhaler.
What are the medications you normally take for your health conditions?
She's on a number of prescriptions, including for high blood pressure, heart disease, and her COPD.
Are there known allergies or medications you react to?
She is allergic to Haldol, Seroquel, and Bactrim.
When was the last time you ate or drank anything?
I had tea with breakfast around 8 am this morning.
You mentioned a past stroke. Do you have any lasting effects from that now?
I had slurred speech and right-sided weakness at the time of the stroke.
You have also mentioned some dizziness this morning. How sudden was that?
It came all on at once. It wasn't gradual at all.
On a scale from 0 to 10, how would you rate your pain now?
10. It's sharp and unrelenting on the left side.
Does anything make it worse, or bring relief at all?
Sitting still helps some, but deep breaths and movement makes it worse.
Is the pain localized or does it travel?
Localized to the left side mostly, no spreading to other areas.
So when exactly did this pain first begin?
It all started around three days ago.
Skin inspection shows no rashes or lesions. Mild redness and swelling in both ankles.
She appears alert and oriented x4. No clear indications of confusion or acute mental status change.
Blood pressure is 174/79 currently.
Respiratory rate is 24 per minute.
Pulse is 75 and regular via radial.
Pulse ox is 94% on room air.
After reassessment, oxygen saturation dropped slightly to 89% on room air.
EKG reveals sinus rhythm with occasional ventricular ectopy.
She is aql (alert and oriented ×4) with a GCS (Glasgow Coma Scale) of 15.
Let's place her on continuous cardiac monitoring now.
Four-lead monitoring is applied now, and rhythm is stable.
A 12-lead EKG is also being applied for a more comprehensive view of her cardiac status.
The 12-lead has been completed. Shows sinus rhythm, no ST changes, and right ventricular conduction delay noted.
End-tidal CO2 shows a shark fin waveform with an approximate reading of 40mmHg.
Glucose could not be measured during this call.
Her oxygen saturation is spiking up to 99% shortly after nebulizer administration.
Based on her symptoms and vital signs, we are proceeding with protocol 3-11 for Respiratory Distress.
We note pallor is not observed today. Her skin coloration remains pink.
To assist with her breathing, we'll administer a nebulizer treatment now.
Her vital signs have improved post nebulizer. Her SpO2 is now 89% but working stable.
We've administered 5mg of Albuterol and 0.5mg of Ipratropium via nebulizer using a viral filter.
She's currently at rest with breath sounds that are clearer now. She appears more stable.
BP is 148/81 and pulse is 74. She's more comfortable after the nebulizer.
After a further 20 minutes, SpO2 has increased to 99% on room air, and breath sounds are adequately clearing.
Is the pain still 10 out of 10, or has there been any improvement?
It's still a 10, but it doesn't feel as sharp now. I feel slightly better.
That's a good sign. Let's continue to keep an eye on that and her comfort.
Mrs. Thompson, do you have a preferred hospital for care?
She's from Boxwood, and they usually transport to [COMPANY_NAME].
We will head to [COMPANY_NAME] Hospital for further assessment and care.
We're ready to carefully transfer her now and secure her onto the stretcher.
She is now secured with full straps and comfortable for transport.
Mrs. Thompson has been moved safely into the ER bed. Handoff has been completed with medical staff.
An RN's signature was obtained for the record due to her right-sided deficit from her prior stroke.
All procedures, findings, and reassessments are fully documented in the ePCR.
